titleStmt.title: Gertrud Oppel
born Herischdorf, Silesia May 3, 1881; died Lugano, August 4, 1925

publicationStmt.publisher: Schenker Documents Online in association with the Centre for Computing in the Humanities

sourceDesc.p: No source: created in electronic format.

encodingDesc.p: Encoding according to the CCH TEI Guidelines

div.p: Gertrud Oppel, was born Gertrud Bandelow in 1881 in the Silesian town of Herischdorf, then part of Prussia (now Jelenia Góra, in Poland). Her family belonged to minor aristocracy and her parents, Friedrich and Louise Bandelow, were wealthy. Gertrud, the third of six children, was a singer and painter; she and the composer and music theorist Reinhard Oppel met first in 1907, when the latter’s first marriage, to Anna Ernestine Catharina (“Käthe”) Brebeck (1884–1955), was already failing, and the two maintained contact (an account of their courtship is given by Reinhard in a letter to his friend Adolf Watzinger dated May 8, 1911 [Reinhard Oppel Archive].) Following the divorce of Reinhard and Käthe on March 17, 1910, he and Gertrud married on May 15, 1911.

With Reinhard, Gertrud had three children: Ulrich (b. June 2, 1910), Walter (b. August 10, 1913; d. 1947), and Hans Reinhard (b. May 14, 1915; March 1990). Gertrud showed early signs of illness in January 1923, and was hospitalized in Kiel in January 1924, where she failed to improve; in October 1924 she was moved to a sanatorium in Lugano, Switzerland, where she died on August 4, 1925, the cause of her death being unclear.

Gertrud figures frequently in Reinhard’s letters to Schenker, particularly in 1923–26. During the later months of her illness, Hans Reinhard was in the care of Gertrud’s sister in Münster, while Ulrich and Walter remained in the family home in Kiel, in the care of a maid whenever Reinhard was in Leipzig. On the first anniversary of her death, he wrote: “A year ago today, I arrived in Lugano to see that no star was shining. And even until now I have not been at peace, my normal self; and I work only from a sense of duty, almost without any joy. Even now I am still always rebelling against fate.” (OJ 13/10, [36]). Reinhard composed an a capella Mass in her memory, which he tried unsuccessfully to persuade Furtwängler to perform.

div.head: Correspondence, Photography
div.p: No letters to Schenker, either from her or including her, survive among the extensive Oppel correspondence. One photograph of her is preserved: dated July 1917, it shows her seated in an armchair in a garden (OJ 72/11, item 2), and other photographs are preserved in the Reinhard Oppel Archive.

bibl.ref: Michael Heymel, Reinhard Oppel, in Lexikon verfolgter Musiker und Musikerinnen der NS-Zeit (LexM), Online-Lexikon des Musikwissenschaftlichen Instituts der Universität Hamburg

listBibl.bibl: Documents and photographs in the Oppel Archive
